title: The case for paying tradespeople more than desk workers
summary: Argue whether skilled manual trades — plumbing, welding, electrical work — should be paid better than entry-level office work, and whether failing to do so distorts a society.
topic: Whether skilled tradespeople should be paid better than entry-level office workers
prompt: Write a 350–500-word argumentative essay about whether skilled tradespeople — plumbers, electricians, welders, motorbike mechanics — should be paid more than entry-level office workers in cities such as Manila, Cairo, or Mumbai. Use a 'what' cleft, a concession ('Granted, …' or 'Admittedly, …'), at least one passive construction, and one participle clause. Anchor the argument in at least one specific city, trade, or economic pattern. Take a clear position.
hintStarters: What a society quietly says about its values when it pays a junior office assistant more than a journeyman electrician is something most…
Granted, the relative wages of trades and entry-level office work reflect broader labour-market forces that no single employer can…
Spend an afternoon in the back streets of Quiapo in Manila and you will see, in plain view, how much skill is required to rewire a building older than its own…

hintVocab.meaningVi: thợ lành nghề (đã qua học việc)
công việc văn phòng cấp khởi điểm

hintVocab.phrase: to be paid in proportion to skill
hintVocab.meaningVi: được trả lương tương xứng với kỹ năng
phụ phí cho lao động cổ cồn trắng
lương thực tế (đã trừ lạm phát)
tình trạng thiếu hụt kỹ năng
đánh giá thấp công việc thể chất
học việc dưới quyền một bậc thầy
công việc bảo trì không thể thiếu
yêu cầu quá nhiều bằng cấp một cách không cần thiết

sampleText:
In most large cities, a junior office assistant with a bachelor's degree and six months' experience is paid more than a fully qualified electrician with twelve years of training and a list of clients who depend on him to keep their buildings safe. This is a fact about labour markets. It is also a fact about how a society has decided to think about skill. The two are not the same, and the second is worth examining.

What a society quietly says about its values when it pays a junior office assistant more than a journeyman electrician is something most people prefer not to make explicit. The implicit message is that proximity to a screen is worth more than competence with one's hands. A young person watching this from the outside reads it correctly: the way to be respected is to leave the trades and find a desk. The trades shrink. The skills shortage that everyone complains about a decade later was, in this sense, paid for in advance.

Granted, the relative wages of trades and entry-level office work reflect broader labour-market forces that no single employer can simply rewrite. Bachelor's degrees are oversupplied, and credential inflation has pushed routine paperwork into roles that once required no degree at all. Trades pay is held down in many cities by informal labour, weak licensing, and the perception that physical work is something one does only until something better appears. These are real forces. The wage gap is not, in most cases, the personal fault of any one office manager.

Even so, structural forces are not exempt from moral evaluation. What a city such as Manila or Cairo loses when it cannot retain young people in welding, plumbing, motorcycle repair, or fine electrical work is not measured in any single firm's accounts. It is measured, eventually, in buildings that fail their inspections, in vehicles that break down on the way to work, and in older tradesmen who train no successors because nobody under thirty is willing to spend a decade learning. The trade leaves the city, and the city pays for that exit, slowly, for the next forty years.

Admittedly, simply raising trade wages by decree does not work; markets adjust, and the increase often ends up in higher prices for the people who can least afford new wiring. What does work is more layered: stronger vocational schools, formal apprenticeship pathways with paid stipends, public projects that pay properly for skilled trade labour, and an honest cultural shift in how the work is described to teenagers. Each of these has been tried, in some form, in cities serious about retaining their trades.

The case for paying tradespeople better is not nostalgic. It is, in the end, a case for taking seriously the people on whom every working city quietly depends — and refusing to let an inherited prejudice about hands and screens decide who eats well.

sampleAnnotations.phrase: This is a fact about labour markets. It is also a fact about how a society has decided to think about skill
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: Đây là một sự thật về thị trường lao động. Đó cũng là một sự thật về cách một xã hội đã quyết định suy nghĩ về kỹ năng

sampleAnnotations.phrase: What a society quietly says about its values when it pays a junior office assistant more than a journeyman electrician is something most people prefer not to make explicit
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: Điều mà một xã hội lặng lẽ nói về các giá trị của nó khi nó trả một trợ lý văn phòng cấp khởi điểm nhiều hơn một thợ điện lành nghề là điều mà hầu hết mọi người thích không làm rõ

sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: Sự thiếu hụt kỹ năng mà mọi người than phiền một thập kỷ sau đó, theo nghĩa này, đã được trả giá từ trước

sampleAnnotations.phrase: the relative wages of trades and entry-level office work reflect broader labour-market forces that no single employer can simply rewrite
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: tiền lương tương đối của các nghề thủ công và công việc văn phòng cấp khởi điểm phản ánh các lực thị trường lao động rộng lớn hơn mà không một người sử dụng lao động đơn lẻ nào có thể đơn giản viết lại

sampleAnnotations.phrase: credential inflation has pushed routine paperwork into roles that once required no degree at all

sampleAnnotations.phrase: Trades pay is held down in many cities by informal labour, weak licensing, and the perception that physical work is something one does only until something better appears
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: Tiền lương nghề thủ công bị giữ ở mức thấp trong nhiều thành phố bởi lao động phi chính thức, sự cấp phép yếu, và quan niệm rằng công việc thể chất là điều người ta chỉ làm cho đến khi có điều gì tốt hơn xuất hiện

sampleAnnotations.phrase: the perception that physical work is something one does only until something better appears
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: quan niệm rằng công việc thể chất là điều người ta chỉ làm cho đến khi có điều gì tốt hơn xuất hiện

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'What' cleft to make an implicit social judgement visible
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What a society quietly says about its values when it pays a junior office assistant more than a journeyman electrician is something most people prefer not to make explicit' dùng cấu trúc 'What … is …' để biến một thông điệp xã hội ngầm thành chủ thể của câu. Cấu trúc này cho phép tác giả đặt một phán xét vào vị trí trung tâm mà không cần đến chủ ngữ trực tiếp.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What a society quietly says about its values when it pays a junior office assistant more than a journeyman electrician is something most people prefer not to make explicit' uses a what-cleft to turn an unspoken social message into the subject of the sentence. The structure lets the writer place a judgement at the centre of the sentence without naming a direct subject.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Passive with foregrounded subject for moral emphasis
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Trades pay is held down in many cities by informal labour, weak licensing, and the perception that physical work is something one does only until something better appears' dùng cấu trúc bị động ('is held down') để đặt 'trades pay' (đối tượng bị tác động) ở vị trí chủ ngữ, và liệt kê các tác nhân ở cuối câu. Cách viết này nhấn mạnh rằng tiền lương bị kìm hãm, và tách bạch ba nguyên nhân khác nhau.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Trades pay is held down in many cities by informal labour, weak licensing, and the perception that physical work is something one does only until something better appears' uses the passive ('is held down') to place 'trades pay' (the thing acted upon) in subject position and lists the agents at the end. The structure emphasises that wages are being held down, and disentangles three different causes.

sampleTranslationVi:
Trong hầu hết các thành phố lớn, một trợ lý văn phòng cấp khởi điểm với bằng cử nhân và sáu tháng kinh nghiệm được trả nhiều hơn một thợ điện đã có đầy đủ chứng chỉ với mười hai năm đào tạo và một danh sách khách hàng phụ thuộc vào ông để giữ cho các tòa nhà của họ an toàn. Đây là một sự thật về thị trường lao động. Đó cũng là một sự thật về cách một xã hội đã quyết định suy nghĩ về kỹ năng. Hai điều đó không giống nhau, và điều thứ hai đáng để xem xét.

Điều mà một xã hội lặng lẽ nói về các giá trị của nó khi nó trả một trợ lý văn phòng cấp khởi điểm nhiều hơn một thợ điện lành nghề là điều mà hầu hết mọi người thích không làm rõ. Thông điệp ngầm là sự gần gũi với màn hình có giá trị hơn năng lực bằng đôi tay của mình. Một người trẻ quan sát điều này từ bên ngoài đọc đúng nó: cách để được tôn trọng là rời khỏi nghề thủ công và tìm một cái bàn làm việc. Các nghề thủ công co lại. Sự thiếu hụt kỹ năng mà mọi người than phiền một thập kỷ sau đó, theo nghĩa này, đã được trả giá từ trước.

Thừa nhận rằng, tiền lương tương đối của các nghề thủ công và công việc văn phòng cấp khởi điểm phản ánh các lực thị trường lao động rộng lớn hơn mà không một người sử dụng lao động đơn lẻ nào có thể đơn giản viết lại. Bằng cử nhân bị cung cấp quá nhiều, và lạm phát bằng cấp đã đẩy công việc giấy tờ thường nhật vào các vị trí mà trước đây không cần bằng cấp gì. Tiền lương nghề thủ công bị giữ ở mức thấp trong nhiều thành phố bởi lao động phi chính thức, sự cấp phép yếu, và quan niệm rằng công việc thể chất là điều người ta chỉ làm cho đến khi có điều gì tốt hơn xuất hiện. Đây là những lực thực sự. Khoảng cách lương, trong hầu hết các trường hợp, không phải là lỗi cá nhân của bất kỳ người quản lý văn phòng nào.

Dẫu vậy, các lực cấu trúc không được miễn khỏi sự đánh giá về mặt đạo đức. Điều mà một thành phố như Manila hay Cairo mất đi khi không thể giữ chân người trẻ trong nghề hàn, ống nước, sửa xe máy, hay điện tinh xảo không được đo lường trong sổ sách của bất kỳ doanh nghiệp đơn lẻ nào. Nó được đo lường, cuối cùng, trong những tòa nhà không qua kiểm tra, trong những phương tiện hỏng dọc đường đi làm, và trong những người thợ lớn tuổi không đào tạo người kế nhiệm vì không ai dưới ba mươi sẵn lòng dành một thập kỷ để học. Nghề thủ công rời khỏi thành phố, và thành phố trả giá cho sự ra đi đó, một cách chậm chạp, trong bốn mươi năm tiếp theo.

Thừa nhận rằng, đơn giản nâng lương nghề thủ công bằng sắc lệnh không hoạt động; thị trường điều chỉnh, và mức tăng thường rơi vào giá cao hơn cho những người ít có khả năng chi trả nhất cho việc đi dây mới. Điều thực sự hoạt động là nhiều lớp hơn: các trường nghề mạnh hơn, các con đường học việc chính thức với trợ cấp có lương, các dự án công trả đúng mức cho lao động nghề lành nghề, và một sự chuyển dịch văn hóa trung thực trong cách công việc được mô tả cho thanh thiếu niên. Mỗi điều trong số này đã được thử nghiệm, dưới hình thức nào đó, trong các thành phố nghiêm túc về việc giữ chân các nghề thủ công của mình.

Lập luận về việc trả lương tốt hơn cho thợ thủ công không phải là hoài niệm. Cuối cùng, đó là một lập luận về việc coi trọng những người mà mỗi thành phố vận hành đều âm thầm phụ thuộc vào — và từ chối để một định kiến kế thừa về đôi tay và màn hình quyết định ai được ăn no.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a 'what' cleft to make a hidden social judgement visible?
options: Most people prefer not to make explicit what a society quietly says about its values journeyman.
What a society quietly says about its values when it pays a junior office assistant more than a journeyman electrician is something most people prefer not to make explicit.
About its values most people prefer what a society quietly says is something not to make explicit a junior office assistant.
A journeyman electrician what a society pays a junior office assistant more than is something most people prefer not.
mcQuestions.explanation: The cleft 'What … is …' lifts the implicit judgement into the subject position and places the writer's evaluation in the emphatic end position.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'What … is …' with the writer's diagnosis placed after 'is'.

options: An increase in the price of issuing official documents
A trend in which more formal qualifications are required for jobs that previously required fewer
An economic inflation that primarily affects bank notes
A rise in the cost of professional registration fees
mcQuestions.explanation: Credential inflation describes the slow rise in the qualifications required to enter or remain in a job — degrees demanded for work that once needed none, masters' degrees pushing out bachelors, and so on.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'inflation' as 'getting bigger' — applied to the credentials needed for a given job.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'granted' correctly as a concession?
options: Granted, the relative wages of trades and entry-level office work reflect broader labour-market forces.
Reflect broader labour-market forces granted the relative wages trades entry-level office work.
Labour-market forces broader granted reflect trades and entry-level office work the relative wages.
Trades and entry-level office work granted broader labour-market forces the relative wages reflect.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted,' followed by a complete clause cleanly grants a real point to the opposing view.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option places 'Granted,' before a full, grammatical sentence?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a passive construction to foreground that wages are being suppressed?
options: Trades pay is held down in many cities by informal labour, weak licensing, and the perception that physical work is something one does only until something better appears.
Informal labour holds down trades pay in many cities weak licensing perception physical work better something only does until appears.
Down trades pay holds informal labour many cities in by weak licensing better something only until physical work does perception appears.
Trades pay better something only does until appears in many cities is held down weak licensing perception physical work informal labour.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'is held down' is the passive verb; 'trades pay' sits in subject position, foregrounding the wages being suppressed and listing the forces afterwards.
mcQuestions.hint: Which sentence puts 'trades pay' in subject position with a passive verb?

options: Reduction of cash wages by withholding tax
A narrowing of the difference in pay between low-paid and high-paid jobs in a sector
Pressing wages into envelopes by hand
An economic recession that reduces all wages equally
mcQuestions.explanation: Wage compression is the narrowing of pay differentials — for example, when skilled and unskilled wages move close together, weakening the financial incentive to acquire skill.
mcQuestions.hint: What does 'compression' do to a range of numbers — spread them out or push them together?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option best paraphrases 'The skills shortage that everyone complains about a decade later was, in this sense, paid for in advance'?
options: Society receives a bill for ignoring skilled trades only ten years after the underpayment begins.
Skills shortages are caused by economic recessions, not by wage decisions.
Everyone has to pay for trade school in advance for a decade.
There is no skills shortage; everyone simply complains about it.
mcQuestions.explanation: The metaphor 'paid for in advance' means the future shortage is the delayed bill for present-day wage decisions that discourage young people from entering the trades.
mcQuestions.hint: What is being 'paid' and when does the bill arrive?

options: The salary attached to a particular job
The social status and respect associated with doing a particular kind of work
The number of qualifications needed for a profession
The ranking of professions by their length of training
mcQuestions.explanation: Occupational prestige is the social standing of a job — the respect it commands — which often runs differently from the income the job provides.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'prestige' as 'respect' — applied to what one does for a living.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a participle clause to describe consequences for the city?
options: In buildings that fail their inspections, in vehicles that break down on the way to work, and in older tradesmen who train no successors.
Inspections that fail in buildings, vehicles that on the way to work break down, and successors who train no older tradesmen.
Older tradesmen no successors that train buildings inspections that fail vehicles break down on the way to work in.
On the way to work break down vehicles in inspections that buildings fail train no successors older tradesmen.
mcQuestions.explanation: Each phrase uses a defining relative clause ('that fail…', 'that break down…', 'who train…') to compress a consequence into a noun phrase — a B2 compression pattern.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for three noun phrases each followed by a relative clause that adds essential detail.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best signals a return to the main argument after a concession?
options: For example,
Even so, structural forces are not exempt from moral evaluation.
On the contrary, structural forces have no consequences.
Furthermore, all forces are moral.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so' grants the concession (market forces are real) and pivots to the writer's main argument that structural forces still demand moral judgement.
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrase both accepts the concession and reasserts the writer's position?

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's central position?
options: All wages should be set equal by law.
Trade wages should be raised by government decree immediately.
Skilled trades should be paid better, supported by stronger vocational schools, formal paid apprenticeships, public projects that pay properly, and an honest cultural shift about manual work.
Office work should be abolished in favour of manual work.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay rejects both pure laissez-faire and a wage decree, proposing instead a layered set of policies plus a cultural shift.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at paragraph five for the writer's specific proposals.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence maintains the most formal academic register?
options: Honestly plumbers are way more useful than office assistants and should get paid more, full stop.
It's kind of crazy that we pay screen people more than hand people, no question.

The persistent underpayment of skilled manual trades relative to entry-level office work reflects a cultural assessment of skill that does not align with the actual operational needs of a working city.
Tradespeople basically run the city and everybody knows it, but nobody pays them right.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C uses formal vocabulary ('persistent underpayment', 'cultural assessment of skill', 'operational needs') and avoids contractions, slang, and informal intensifiers.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to dignify a trade' mean?
options: To officially register a trade with a guild
To treat a trade with the social respect that its skill and contribution deserve
To pay a trade so well that no one will need to do other work
To restrict a trade so that only qualified people may practise it
mcQuestions.explanation: To dignify a trade is to grant it the respect — in language, pay, training, and cultural framing — that the work itself warrants.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'dignify' as 'give dignity to' — what would that look like for a trade?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses a defining relative clause?
options: Older tradesmen who train no successors because nobody under thirty is willing to spend a decade learning.
Who train no successors older tradesmen because nobody under thirty is willing to spend a decade learning.
Because nobody under thirty willing is to spend a decade learning older tradesmen who train no successors.
Learning a decade older tradesmen who under thirty nobody willing to spend train no successors because is.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'who train no successors because nobody under thirty is willing to spend a decade learning' is a defining relative clause specifying which older tradesmen the essay means.
mcQuestions.hint: Defining relative clauses follow the noun directly and start with 'who', 'that', or 'which'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'an inherited prejudice about hands and screens' mean?
options: A medical condition affecting people who work with computers
A long-standing cultural assumption — passed down rather than thought through — that work done with screens is more valuable than work done with hands
A bias toward screen-based technology in interior design
A formal classification of jobs by hand or screen use
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Inherited' marks this as a cultural assumption passed down from older generations rather than reasoned out; the essay is asking the reader to examine what they inherited rather than what they decided.
mcQuestions.hint: What is being inherited, and why does the essay say it should be reconsidered?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best closes the essay by reframing the wage question as a moral one?
options: In conclusion, both office workers and tradespeople are valuable.
Therefore, all office work should be banned tomorrow.

The case for paying tradespeople better is not nostalgic. It is, in the end, a case for taking seriously the people on whom every working city quietly depends — and refusing to let an inherited prejudice about hands and screens decide who eats well.
To sum up, wages are a complicated subject with no easy answers.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C states the essay's position precisely, names the bad inheritance, and ends with a sharp image ('who eats well') — a strong rhetorical close.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option states a sharp moral claim rather than a vague summary?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay say a wage decree alone does NOT solve?
options: Whether trades are taught in formal apprenticeships
The risk that increased wages flow into higher prices for people who can least afford the work
The cultural assumption that office work is more respectable
All of the above — and the essay names this as the reason wage decrees alone fail
mcQuestions.explanation: Paragraph five names exactly these complications: a decree without supporting infrastructure leads to price pass-throughs, leaves training systems unchanged, and does not shift the cultural framing.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at paragraph five for the writer's specific critique of decree-only solutions.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of a tradesperson whose work has affected your daily life recently — the plumber who fixed a leak, the mechanic who got your motorbike running, the electrician who rewired a flat. What were they paid relative to a junior office assistant in your city? What does that comparison say about your city's actual values, as opposed to its stated ones?
